# Asset reference — voice and face inputs

Read this when running asset selection in Phase 1. Show the user the relevant format expectations **before** they pick so they know whether a given file will actually work.

## Voice reference (Qwen3-TTS clone source)

**What it controls:** the *timbre and prosody* of the narrator. Every slide's audio is generated by feeding this reference + its exact transcript into Qwen3-TTS along with the slide's `speaker_notes`.

**Expected format:**

| Property | Required value |
|---|---|
| Container | `.wav` |
| Sample rate | 24 000 Hz |
| Channels | mono |
| Bit depth | 16-bit PCM |
| Duration | 12–18 seconds (sweet spot ~14s) |
| Content | one continuous clip of the target speaker talking naturally |
| Loudness | normalized to roughly `-20 LUFS` (`loudnorm I=-20:TP=-2:LRA=11`) |
| Sidecar | a `.txt` file with the **exact** spoken words, same basename |

**Critical:** the `.txt` transcript must match the audio word-for-word, including real filler words ("uh", "right") if the speaker actually said them. **Do not** insert synthetic tags like `[pause]`.

**Impact of choice:**

- Calmer, slower reference → calmer, slower narration (sometimes too slow for technical content).
- Energetic / sales-y reference → narration sounds promotional even on technical slides.
- Reference with background noise → audible artifacts in every slide.
- Reference shorter than ~12s → less stable voice match.
- Reference longer than ~18s → no quality gain, slower generation.

**Make a new one from a longer recording:**

```bash
ffmpeg -y -ss <start_sec> -t 14.0 -i <source_file> \
  -af "loudnorm=I=-20:TP=-2:LRA=11" \
  -ac 1 -ar 24000 -sample_fmt s16 \
  assets/voice_refs/<name>.wav
# then write assets/voice_refs/<name>.txt with the exact spoken words
```

Cut a **middle** segment, not the first few seconds — recordings usually start with throat-clearing or mic-setup artifacts.

## Face reference (Wav2Lip source video)

**What it controls:** the *visible person* in the talking-head video. Wav2Lip preserves the source face and repaints only the mouth region driven by the audio.

**Expected format:**

| Property | Required value |
|---|---|
| Container | `.mp4` (H.264 / yuv420p) |
| Resolution | 1920×1080 (smaller works but PiP quality degrades) |
| Frame rate | 30 fps |
| Duration | 8–12 seconds (loops if slide is longer) |
| Content | one person, single stable face, looking at the camera, mouth visible and moving naturally |
| Audio track | not required — Wav2Lip ignores it |

**Critical:** pick a **stable middle segment** with no big head movement, no occlusion of the mouth, and consistent lighting. The first few seconds of any recording almost always contain setup motion — avoid them, otherwise the lip-sync output shows obvious motion loops.

**Impact of choice:**

- Stable face, mouth clearly visible → cleanest lip sync.
- Person turning head / looking away → Wav2Lip's face detector loses lock, mouth region renders dark or grey.
- Strong shadows on lower face → low-res 96×96 mouth crop visibly mismatches the rest of the face.
- Multiple faces in frame → Wav2Lip picks one nondeterministically.
- 720p source → final PiP looks soft; 1080p is the floor.

**Make a new one from a longer recording:**

```bash
ffmpeg -y -ss <start_sec> -t 10 -i <source_file> \
  -vf "fps=30,scale=1920:1080:flags=lanczos,format=yuv420p" \
  -c:v libx264 -preset slow -crf 18 -an -movflags +faststart \
  assets/face_reference_<name>.mp4
```

After producing it, eyeball the file before using it for a full deck:

```bash
open assets/face_reference_<name>.mp4
```

**Cache caveat:** Wav2Lip caches face boxes per face-ref filename at `outputs/<run>/cache/lipsync/<basename>.pk`. If you reuse a filename for a different person/clip, the old face boxes apply silently. Either use a new filename or delete the stale `.pk` file before the next run.

## Speaker style (`assets/speaker_style.txt`)

**What it controls:** the *word choices and sentence shapes* of `speaker_notes` — i.e., what the narrator actually says, not how the voice sounds. The `SpeakerStyleAgent` rewrites generated notes to match this style before TTS runs.

**Expected format:** 4–8 sentences in the presenter's natural speaking voice — drawn from real meetings, slack messages, recorded explanations. Plain text. Not a polished mission statement.

**Impact of choice:**

- Replace the placeholder example file → narration sounds like the presenter.
- Leave the placeholder → narration sounds like generic corporate-trainer English (technically correct, recognizably not the presenter).
- Style sentences from a different domain than the presentation → narration may pick up odd analogies; usually still better than the placeholder.

## How the three combine

| Asset | Affects |
|---|---|
| `speaker_style.txt` | the **words** said on each slide |
| voice ref `.wav` + `.txt` | the **voice timbre and prosody** of those words |
| face ref `.mp4` | the **person you see** on the PiP video, and the lip sync quality |

All three matter. A perfect voice clone speaking generic corporate English with a flat face reference still looks off. State this to the user when they're picking — it justifies the time spent on selection.
